Hey, Dirk. Hello, Dasz. Hello, Martin. John. Good to see you all. Great. We are going to work on another collaboration session today. I see it's a small group this morning. Everybody is still recovering from their eastern celebrations, probably. Well, let's dive into it. So last time we had an in-depth teaching session about reciprocity. And I will recap the lessons learned there in a second. So, collaboration sessions. I know most of you already participated in previous collaboration sessions. So what we do during the sessions, you will go help each other solving your influence challenges. And the beauty of it is, especially when you're not in the industry, it's not your day-to-day challenge. You have a much more creative view on helping others solving these challenges based on the principles of influence, because these are universal principles of influence. So, often when you have a current challenge and you think about it all day, you form some kind of blinders. With the help of others, they remove those blinders, so to say, and often see we'll have much more effective outcomes to move forward. So, we always use the influence process to do these collaboration sessions in groups of three. Everybody will help each other and you get 30 minutes per case. A quick recap of the influence process. So, Dr. Cialdini not only discovered the seven universal principles of influence, but he also created a highly effective process on how to use them in the most efficient time way to get the most effective and ethical outcomes to overcome your influence challenges. So, what we are going to do when you enter the breakout rooms, the first 10 seconds you announce a timekeeper. That's a very easy one. The second thing you do, you announce the order in which you will help each other. So, one case goes first, one goes second, one goes third, and if you are with four people, one goes as fourth. How does this process work? Just to recap, often when we start a brainstorm session, people just put a problem in the room and people just start shouting and you get a competition who gets the loudest voice. That's not very effective. And the first step is probably the most important one, because often when people address a problem, the rest of the room understands it for 85, 90 percent, but the 10 percent will lead to a huge differentiator in how people perceive the challenge and therefore what kind of solutions they will bring to the table. So, step back is the first step and step back is clearly address what is the challenge and what do you hope to accomplish. To do that, we always answer five questions. What is your current situation? Who is involved? What do you hope to accomplish? What barriers you see and what are potential solutions? What are solutions you already tried or thinking about? By answering these five questions, people have a much more comprehensive view of the challenge. But it is simple, step back cannot take more than five minutes. So, the timekeeper, the person that goes first, have five minutes to address that challenge based on answering those five questions. And then something very special will happen in the breakout rooms. Instead of directly jumping on a problem, we do something that we should do more often. We start thinking about the solution instead of telling people what's the first thing that comes to mind. And if you are with three people in your breakout session, you take seven minutes to think about it. And you can think about all the influence principles, but today we will mainly focus on reciprocity because that was the topic of our last in-depth teaching session. And thinking is an individual activity. So, if we think about sound level, step back, one person is talking, the rest is silence. Thinking, everybody is silent. And that's especially in physical brainstorm sessions. It feels in the beginning when you enter this, when you address or when you start using this process, it feels uncomfortable. People are used to get into a room together and start talking. I promise you, this is much more effective when people think first before they start talking. So, that's what the order is. Then we want solicit input from everybody. Sometimes the cleaning lady could have a better idea on what is the best solution than the CEO of a Fortune One Homo Company, depending on the topic. So, we want to listen to everybody. How do we do that? If you are with three people, people get max two and a half minutes to share their best ideas. If you are with four people in the breakout session, people get max two minutes to share the best ideas which they came up with during their thinking time. This is a listening exercise. So, one person talks, the rest listens. Don't say, oh yes, that's a great idea, but or, well, I don't think this will work. No comments in that phase. Just listening and hearing what the other person brings to the table. So, now we did, let's say you are with three people, five minutes, seven minutes, three times two and a half minutes. That's 12 plus that's 19 and a half minutes. You have 10 and a half minutes left and this is the place where everybody can talk. You know, from all the ideas that you heard, you bundle them and try to prioritize them in the most low hanging fruit, the most easy to apply ideas which you can walk away after 30 minutes with a ready to go action plan which you can start implementing right away. So, what we know, of course, is that in organizations you have hours and hours of brainstorming and often people leave the room with no action plan. We just had a fun talk and there are some ideas on the table, but not what are we going to do here in 30 minutes, you walk away. And this is what we're going to do. And that's the last step. And the last step is also not write extensive reports which nobody reads because there's a problem with no takers and then they email with all the best intentions. Well, here's a 40 page document and this is all what we discussed. Two things happen. One, nobody reads them because we are busy with other things. Second, the person who is drafting and writing these reports gets anchored. That's the solid truth in an influence challenge. This is not black and white magical science. It highly increases the likelihood that you hear yes, but you need to stay flexible in your mind. So you want to have a list of bullet points prioritized idea one we put into action. As per step idea to second, third and so on and that you do in 10 and a half minutes. So for people who are with four people in the breakout session, step back five minutes, five minutes, thinking time, four times two minutes, you have 18 minutes. And then you take seven minutes for creating the plan because then you still have time in one and a half hours to address four cases. That's what we're going to do today. We practice this process. And I see most of you here are also certified coaches. And I always say when I did live training, when I did coaching, coach people to the practitioner program. People say always, wow, this is powerful. I love these principles already got some great ideas. What I also really loved is this influence process because I see how many time it will save me. I see how much more effective our meetings will become. So I definitely would advise you start using this process in exercises, even if it's a preselectivity. It's a very powerful tool to show your value. John, I see you have a question. Yeah, hello, bass. Good morning, everybody. Good morning, wherever you are, it might not be morning. I'm just fascinated by where where does this process come from? Is it your process or is it Dr. Chaldini's or is it based on a combination of a, you know, a number of different research papers or just be interested in where it comes from. It's based on behavioral science research and Dr. Knight and Dr. Chaldini discovered or created this process. And Dr. Knight was actually in the lead of developing this. As you see how they work together for 30 years, it was Dr. Chaldini spread the word. He was often hired for keynotes and some consulting later on. Dr. Knight did most of the consulting in the Fortune 500 slash Fortune 100 companies where they actively worked for. And he noticed that there was a huge problem when people learned his principles and then started brainstorming of the implementation. It took hours and hours and they didn't walk away with the best possible outcomes, first of all, and second, it took way too long. So that was the trigger for him to start developing such a process. So it's they deserve the credits for this process. And your question actually triggers one more thing that I like to explain and that has to do with step two, thinking. It's not just usual brain. I always make a joke. I said thinking seven minutes or five minutes, let's say seven minutes doesn't mean think one minute and stare out the window six minutes. It means usual brain seven minutes, really. But there's also one more important thing. Use it in a structured way. And we have a very systematic approach how you use these principles and the structure and systematic approach is try to recognize signals. So if people don't pick up their phone, they don't reply your email. These are signals. And then you bring back to the core motors model and these signals are connected to you need to work on your relationship. If you know this, then you know you need to work. You need to think of the principle of reciprocity, liking and unity and through these principles, you can think of activators and amplifiers. You can think of a set of ideas. And that's the part you follow all the time in this thinking process if you talk about influence. So there are two crucial steps in step two. It's one, do the thinking before you start talking. And the two things I want to read address was is gift. A gift is a activator of reciprocity. So what amplifies that gift is what is meaningful, unexpected and customized. And I told how can you find those elements where people share all that information nowadays on internet, on their social media profiles. And companies have people profiles on their website. So that only takes normally five to 10 minutes to do that exercise. What is unexpected? Well, that requires some creativity, but we have we always know we are we don't expect people actually coming to help us. We expect people wanting something from us. So that's the mindset. Ask yourself, not the question, how can I move this people to do something for me? If you think about reciprocity, ask yourself the question, what can I do to really help that person? You can trust the science that people reciprocate. And by having the last approach, we know you are much more effective. So that's already an unexpected thing that you really come with the intention to help the other person and customized. I explained, well, customized, good thing. And I give a hand and I give it to you that feels customized is generic, but the feeling is customized. So it's not always you have to go out and do big research and buy something and then hand it over. It can be simplified. Second thing on that gift. And then I think my biggest takeaway what I wanted to address in last in that teaching session. In a reflection, everybody thinks reciprocity is about gift giving. I need to buy something. Then I give it. I just thought that's not true that I intangible gifts. But there's one extra thing we always think on the individual. Often that individual works in an organization or there's a bigger picture. Not only ask yourself the question, what does that individual need? Ask yourself the question, what does the organization need? Research shows for a lot of research shows the organization will act reciprocal. But if the organization has a pressure to ask reciprocal to you. You have much more benefit because that person in the organization could get another job, could get promoted to another department. But if you did something special, you can trust that they will tell their colleagues and suddenly the pressure of reciprocation comes not only at the individual, it comes to the organization. That's a mindset, common mistake that I see often that we only think how can I help that individual start thinking how can I help the bigger picture? How can I help the organization? Yes, John. I hope you don't mind me interrupting. Oh, absolutely not. I just be interested. Could you just give an example of one gift that you could give organizationally to an organization?
